title: TTA in Production Pipelines
concept: Deploying TTA in production requires managing the latency-accuracy tradeoff. While TTA is ideal for offline, high-stakes analysis (like medical imaging), real-time systems (like robotics) usually skip it to maintain speed.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the difference between hard and soft voting in TTA?

options.text: Hard voting averages predicted class names; soft voting averages raw probability vectors
Hard voting is for training; soft voting is for validation
Hard voting uses integers; soft voting uses complex numbers

exercises.explain: Yes! Soft voting averages raw probability vectors, preserving confidence.

cards.title: The Deployment Dilemma
cards.body: TTA consistently adds 1-2% accuracy to a trained model. In Kaggle competitions, everyone turns TTA on to win. But in a real factory running a conveyor belt at 60 frames per second, running a model 5 times per frame is impossible. We must decide if the extra accuracy is worth the slowdown.

cards.title: The Latency Tradeoff
cards.body: TTA multiplies the computational cost of inference by the number of passes ($N$). If your base model takes 20ms to predict, a 5-pass TTA pipeline takes **100ms** (plus the overhead of generating augmentations). TTA is gated by your **latency budget**.

cards.title: The pit stop check
cards.body: In a high-speed Formula 1 race, checking every bolt on the car during a pit stop is too slow (real-time robotics). In a routine annual safety inspection, checking every bolt is essential and time is not a factor (medical diagnostics). TTA is the safety inspection.

cards.title: High-Stakes, Offline Tasks
cards.body: In medical imaging (like skin lesion classification), a patient waits days for results. Latency doesn't matter, but accuracy is life-or-death. We run aggressive, 10-pass TTA pipelines to ensure the safest possible classification.

cards.title: TTA is not a training fix
cards.body: Don't use TTA as a band-aid for a poorly trained model. If your CNN has low capacity or was trained on bad labels, TTA will just average multiple incorrect predictions, doing nothing to solve the root cause. Build a strong base model first.

cards.title: When to Use TTA in Production

left.text:
Medical diagnosis (accuracy first)
Satellite photo analysis
Offline batch sorting

right.text:
Robotics & autonomous driving
Real-time video feeds (60 FPS)
Low-power mobile chips

exercises.prompt: For which production scenario is TTA LEAST appropriate?

options.text: Real-time obstacle detection in a fast-moving autonomous drone
Dermatologist screening tool for skin cancer scans
Analyzing satellite images offline to estimate crop yields
Classifying uploaded product photos on a slow retail website

exercises.explain: Correct! Fast drones have extremely tight latency budgets, making TTA's Nx slowdown dangerous.

exercises.prompt: True or false: TTA is a great substitute for a poorly trained model with corrupted training weights.
exercises.explain: False. TTA only helps a well-trained model find edge cases; it cannot correct fundamentally broken weights.

exercises.sentence: TTA is highly suited for high-stakes, ___ tasks where accuracy matters far more than prediction speed.
exercises.explain: Offline classification (like medical scans) values accuracy over instant latency, making it perfect for TTA.

exercises.prompt: If a base CNN takes 15ms to make a prediction, how long will a 6-pass TTA pipeline approximately take to run?

options.text: Exactly 15ms, because passes run in parallel on CPU registers
Over 5 seconds, due to file storage read constraints

exercises.explain: Correct! Latency scales linearly with the number of passes: 15ms * 6 = 90ms.

exercises.prompt: Your company is deploying a security camera system that must identify license plates. The camera feeds run at 5 FPS. If base model latency is 30ms, can you afford a 5-pass TTA pipeline?

options.text: Yes — 5 passes take ~150ms, which easily fits within the 200ms budget per frame at 5 FPS, boosting reading accuracy
No — because any latency above 10ms causes the camera to disconnect
No — because license plates are letters and cannot be processed by convolutional networks
Yes — because TTA will reduce the latency to exactly 5ms

exercises.explain: Exactly! 150ms fits within the 200ms frame time at 5 FPS. Next: the complete Phase 5 capstone recap!
